question_text: An Australian producer is expanding its bubbly range because customers increasingly want lively, refreshing sparklers, including very low-sugar styles. One lot has already been finished through disgorgement and now tastes drier than the target release. The winemaker also wants this wine to remain distinctly Australian rather than relying on the usual sparkling grapes. Which plan best fits those goals?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Use Pinot Noir and increase sweetness after disgorgement with unfermented grape juice.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Use Shiraz for the sparkling wine and, after disgorgement, fine-tune sweetness with a small addition of wine mixed with sugar syrup.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Use Chardonnay and set the sweetness before disgorgement by adding only sugar syrup.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Use Pinot Meunier and avoid any post-disgorgement adjustment because very dry sparkling wine cannot be modified at that stage.'}]
source_facts:
[1] Whilst most sparkling wine is produced from Chardonnay, Pinot noir and possibly Pinot Meuniere, an Australian speciality is sparkling Shiraz, a red sparkling wine produced from Shiraz grapes.
---
[2] A dosage mixture of fresh wine and some sugar syrup is used to adjust the sweetness level of the wine after it has been disgorged.
---
[3] A rise in consumer demand and an ever-broadening selection of cool climate grapes has led to an increase in fresh and delightful sparkling wines ranging from bone dry extra brut.

question_text: A European producer is finalizing packaging for a new release and wants to go beyond the mandatory labeling elements. options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'All three are optional enhancements; the tactile element follows a precedent set by Chapoutier and now adopted by producers in several countries, the scannable graphic can carry vintage-specific data, and the reverse label customarily holds non-mandatory supplementary content.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'The tactile dots and scannable graphic are optional, but the reverse label content is legally required to appear somewhere on the bottle and must include the vintage date.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': "Only the scannable graphic is optional; tactile elements are now mandatory across the EU following Chapoutier's precedent, and reverse-label food pairing text is regulated."}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'All three must be approved as part of the official label registration because they convey vintage-specific information that falls under mandatory disclosure rules.'}]
source_facts:

[2] In addition to these national labeling requirements producers may include their web site address and a QR Code with vintage specific information.

question_text: A winemaker is managing a historic California estate whose original vineyards were established by 19th-century settlers from Italy. Currently, the estate's flagship red vines are suffering from severe viral infections, a recognized vulnerability stemming from the cultivar's highly inbred genetic history. Because yields of this primary grape are drastically reduced, the winemaker plans to supplement the vintage by incorporating a small fraction of other varieties. They wish to utilize grapes that are traditionally authorized for such mixtures, acknowledging that contemporary vintners usually prefer to maximize the primary grape's concentration. Based on the identity of this flagship vine, which specific varieties should the winemaker source for this blend?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'Sangiovese, Canaiolo, and Colorino'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'Cabernet Sauvignon, Merlot, and Cabernet Franc'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'Dolcetto, Barbera, and Freisa'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'Bonarda, Croatina, and Vespolina'}]
source_facts:
[1] === United States ===

In California, the influence of Italian immigrants in the early history of the state's wine industry introduced Nebbiolo to the United States in the 19th century.
---
[2] Perhaps due to inbreeding in Nebbiolo's lineage, the vine is very prone to grape diseases caused by viruses.
---
[3] Rather than mandate a 100% Nebbiolo, producers are allowed to blend a small percentage of Bonarda, Croatina and Vespolina though most modern producers favour a high percentage of Nebbiolo.

question_text: A New Zealand winemaker is drafting the back-label story for a new release from a small family cellar in Wairarapa. She wants the wording to position the wine within the country's current quality image while also avoiding a misleading impression that her region is dominated by very large companies. Which label statement is the most accurate and persuasive choice?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': "Argue that New Zealand's reputation is driven mostly by large-scale wineries spread evenly across all regions, so boutique producers should avoid highlighting small-estate credentials."}, {'id': 'B', 'text': "Emphasize that New Zealand's best-regarded wines increasingly come from small artisanal estates, note that major producers are concentrated mainly in Marlborough, Hawke's Bay, and Gisborne rather than Wairarapa, and mention that one of Hawke's Bay's historic estates dates to the late 1800s."}, {'id': 'C', 'text': "Describe Hawke's Bay as lacking deep winery history, while claiming that boutique recognition is strongest only in Wairarapa and not a broader national trend."}, {'id': 'D', 'text': "State that Wairarapa is one of the main homes of the country's largest wineries, and cite Mission Estate there as an example of a producer founded in the late 1800s."}]
source_facts:
[1] Wairarapa wine region are located fairly evenly throughout all wine regions, with the larger producers predominantly in Marlborough, Hawke's Bay, Gisborne.
---
[2] As in many places in the world, an emerging trend in New Zealand wine is an increased recognition for high quality wines coming from small boutique wineries.
---
[3] Mission Estate, were established in the Hawke's Bay area in the late 19th century.

question_text: A winemaker in a South American cellar is reviewing a newly acquired old vineyard block. The estate archive says the planting material came with French immigrant growers in the late 1800s. In the winery, the fruit from this block produces a deeply opaque red wine with a firm, grippy structure. Older records and neighboring growers also refer to the same grape under many different local names, creating confusion during replanting plans. Which conclusion best fits all of these clues?
options: [{'id': 'A', 'text': 'The block is probably a recently introduced variety, since grapes producing deeply colored, tannic wines were generally planted much later in South America.'}, {'id': 'B', 'text': 'The block is unlikely to be Malbec because a grape with many alternate names would not also be associated with a single immigrant community or a consistent wine style.'}, {'id': 'C', 'text': 'The block is most likely Malbec, a grape introduced by French settlers in the late nineteenth century, known for very dark wines, strong tannic structure, and an unusually large number of alternate names.'}, {'id': 'D', 'text': 'The records point away from Malbec because French-origin grapes in South America are usually easy to identify by one standard name and are not especially dark or tannic.'}]
source_facts:
[1] Malbec was brought to the area by French settlers at the end of the 19th century.
---
[2] == Synonyms == The French ampelographer Pierre Galet has documented over a thousand different synonyms for Malbec.
---
[3] The grapes tend to have an inky dark color and robust tannins.
